title: What Is My Mum Rage Actually Telling Me I Need?
subtitle: A journal practice for reading maternal rage as information rather than evidence of failure
excerpt: A journal practice for reading mum rage as data about unmet needs rather than evidence of bad character. For the mother who is frightened or ashamed of her anger and wants to understand what it is trying to say.

practice.whenToUse: Use when rage episodes have become frequent or disproportionate, or when you want to understand what the anger is protecting.

content.text: A journal practice for reading mum rage. Maternal rage is among the most under-discussed experiences of motherhood: the disproportionate anger that can arrive over small triggers, the frightening speed and force of it, the shame that follows. This practice works from the premise that the rage is information, not verdict. It is pointing at something that needs attention.
Describe the last significant rage episode factually: what triggered it, what happened, what the aftermath felt like.
Write it plainly. No self-criticism and no defence. Just what occurred.
What need was not being met in the hours or days before the rage arrived?
Sleep, rest, time alone, being heard, physical space, food, not being touched, a task left unfinished. Write what was building underneath.
What did the rage want that the trigger couldn't actually give?
Rage at a spilled cup is usually not about the cup. What was it actually asking for? Recognition, rest, help, space? Write the real request underneath the outburst.
What is one thing that, if it were in place regularly, would reduce the build-up?
Not a complete solution. One thing. Write what it is and whether it is currently possible.
